Why this exists

Neither engine behind this page was built for terrain. My geospatial platform was built for real-estate market data: county records ingested into a hashed, auditable ledger and served read-only. My geometry engine was built from first principles in a bare terminal with nothing but C++ and g++ installed (low-level predicates, triangulation, Voronoi, no libraries), to see whether I could. Neither had anything to do with mapping ground.

This page is what happened when I pointed them at real ground anyway. Public USGS elevation goes in; the geometry engine turns it into a triangulated surface; the earthwork and drainage are computed on that surface; a real lot boundary would come from the platform. Empty drawing board to a working, genuinely useful tool in about an hour and a half, and nearly all of it ran on my own machine on my own code (the public elevation data and the plotting aside). That speed comes from building each piece properly the first time, so two of them bolt together into something new and still hold.

Terrain & earthwork · three studies

What can the engines really do?

What those two engines do once they are pointed at real ground. First, a single building lot: how much dirt to cut a level pad. Then a whole hillside: where a storm's rain runs off. Then a Sierra canyon closed by a dam, and the reservoir it would hold.

Study 1 · Building padFoothill lot · Fresno County · 37.08°N 119.49°W

Imagine you're a developer sizing up this foothill lot to build on. First you need a level pad, which on a slope means cutting the high side down and filling the low side up. Look at the ground and you can't tell how much dirt that takes, or whether the cut balances the fill. That number is most of the grading bill, and it's the first thing you'd want to know: what does a level pad here cost in dirt?

39 ft
Fall across lot
12.9 %
Steepest grade
2,089 ft
Balanced pad
18,320 yd³
Earth moved
~0 yd³
Hauled off-site

The pad

You cut the high side down and fill the low side up until it's level. Put the pad at the balanced grade (2,089 ft) and the dirt you remove matches the dirt you need, so almost nothing is trucked in or hauled off. On a slope, that difference is most of the grading bill.

The dirt

Making the pad moves about 18,320 cubic yards (roughly 9,160 out, 9,160 back in), so off-site haul is essentially nothing. Every number traces to a measured point on the ground.

The water

The lot drains southwest and collects at the low corner. Put a swale or drain there before you pour; it's cheaper than fixing it after the first storm.

Study 2 · RainwaterWest bluff · San Joaquin River rim · north Fresno

Imagine you're the engineer siting houses and drainage along this bluff, which drops 95 feet to the San Joaquin and sheds water fast in a storm. From the surface you can't see where that water goes, but where it gathers is exactly what erodes and what you have to design around. So: where does the rain actually run?

95 ft
Fall down bluff
39 %
Steepest grade
NW
Bluff faces
604k gal
Runoff · 1-in storm · 22 ac
WNW
Drains to river

The slope

A steep west-northwest-facing bluff dropping 95 feet toward the San Joaquin, 39% at the steepest.

The runoff

There's no holding this water in place; it runs off the face. A 1-inch storm sheds roughly 604,000 gallons off the modeled slope, all of it running downhill.

Where it goes

The runoff gathers into a draw that cuts northwest down the bluff and exits toward the river. Build near the rim and that draw is the line to respect: it's where the water and the erosion go.

Study 3 · ReservoirSan Joaquin River gorge · Sierra Nevada · 37.03°N 119.57°W

Imagine you're a water agency weighing whether to dam this dry Sierra gorge. It narrows to a single outlet on the south; wall that outlet and the canyon fills. A dam is one of the most expensive things a public agency builds, and the number that decides whether the site is even worth surveying is this one: dam the outlet, and how much would it hold?

44,457 ac-ft
Full-pool capacity
256 ft
Dam height
449 ac
Surface at full pool
14.5 B gal
Water impounded
0.9 mi
Reservoir reach

The basin

A dry Sierra gorge that falls to a single narrow outlet on the south. Close that notch with a 256-foot dam and the canyon behind it becomes a reservoir, held on the other three sides by its own rim.

The water it holds

At full pool the basin holds 44,457 acre-feet, about 14.5 billion gallons. The stage–storage curve says what each extra ten feet of dam buys, so the dam can be sized to the need.

What it is worth

This is the first-pass number a city, county, or irrigation district pays a consultant for: how much a site holds and how tall the dam must be. My engine produces it from public data in an afternoon, before anyone surveys a thing.

How this was made

01 · elevation

Real USGS 10-meter elevation, sampled across each site: 100 points on the pad, 576 on the bluff, and 3,721 across the reservoir basin.

02 · surface

The points are triangulated into a continuous surface (a TIN) by a C++ geometry engine I built from scratch. 162, 1,058, and 7,200 triangles, exact to Euler's formula.

03 · analysis

From that surface: cut and fill balanced to a pad, and rainfall routed downhill neighbor-to-neighbor to find where water concentrates.

County GIS · layer ready

It runs on the same engine as my county GIS platform, so a lot boundary can come from official parcel data, and either study runs on any parcel in the county from its address alone.

A demonstration of the method on real public data, not a licensed survey or construction document. Locations are approximate; satellite imagery courtesy Esri World Imagery. Ten-meter data suits planning and early pricing, and the same pipeline reads a surveyed point cloud without change.
